exampleSentencesTranslated: I must buy a new shirt.

example.vi: Bạn phải làm bài tập về nhà.
example.en: You have to do your homework.

notes:
phải usually means must / have to. It shows obligation, something necessary or required.

It goes before a verb to say what someone is required to do.  

You’ll also see không phải = “not” (it’s not / not the right one), which is very common and useful.  
Another common pattern: phải… mới… = “have to… then…” (phải làm xong mới nghỉ)

Bonus meaning: phải can also mean “right” (opposite of left), like “right side.”
exampleSentences: Em phải đọc cuốn sách này.
Anh phải mua cái áo mới.
notes_md:
**phải** usually means **must / have to**. It shows obligation, something necessary or required.

It goes before a verb to say what someone is required to do.  

You’ll also see **không phải** = “not” (it’s not / not the right one), which is very common and useful.  
Another common pattern: **phải… mới…** = “have to… then…” (phải làm xong mới nghỉ)

Bonus meaning: **phải** can also mean “right” (opposite of left), like “right side.”
sentenceBreakdowns: Em (I) + phải (must) + đọc (read) + cuốn (classifier for books) + sách (book) + này (this)
Anh (I) + phải (must) + mua (buy) + cái (classifier) + áo (shirt) + mới (new)
